deepseek-r1-distill-llama-8b.text: Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this 5-year-old boy with the hyperpigmented macules on his trunk.
Let me start by breaking down the information given.
First, the patient has multiple hyperpigmented macules.
Macules are small, flat, colored patches on the skin, often seen in conditions like vitiligo or post-inflammatory hypopigmentation.
But here, they're hyperpigmented, so maybe something else.
Next, when the doctor rubbed the lesion with the rounded end of a pen, uicarial wheal developed, and it was confined to the border of the lesion.
Uicarial wheals are those pinkish to red, raised patches that appear after scratching, commonly seen in urticaria (hives).
The fact that the wheal is only at the border suggests it's localized to the area around the macules.
Putting this together, the child has hyperpigmented macules that, when rubbed, cause localized urticarial wheals.
This pattern is classic for a condition called urticaria pigmentosa.
I remember that urticaria pigmentosa is a type of physical urticaria where the skin becomes more sensitive, and friction or rubbing can cause these wheals.
It's often associated with hyperpigmentation, especially in areas like the neck, back, and upper trunk, which matches the presentation here.
So, the most likely diagnosis is urticaria pigmentosa.
It's important to consider other possibilities like contact dermatitis or scabies, but the localized wheal response on the border of the macules points more towards urticaria pigmentosa.
The child's presentation of hyperpigmented macules with localized uricarial wheals upon rubbing is indicative of urticaria pigmentosa.
